data.title: Ahold Delhaize: Marginally Better Value Relatative To Kroger
data.description:
Introduction

So far in 2025, Ahold Delhaize (OTCQX:ADRNY) has significantly outperformed peers such as Carrefour (OTCPK:CRRFY) and Kroger (KR), delivering a return of ~32%:

I previously covered Ahold Delhaize with a Buy rating back in March 2024, highlighting its attractive valuation relative to U.S. peers such as Kroger. At the same time, I did note that European peers such as Carrefour offered an even higher free cash flow yield.

In this article, I share my takeaways from Ahold Delhaize's Q1 2025 results and provide an update on valuation relative to Kroger and Carrefour. I confirm my Buy rating for Ahold Delhaize, largely driven by a more attractive valuation relative to Kroger. Even so, I admit that the Buy case has weakened given the strong appreciation of Ahold Delhaize so far in 2025.

Company Overview

You can access all Ahold Delhaize results on its website here. Ahold Delhaize operates 9,555 stores with a focus on food retail under a variety of brands (Food Lion, Hannaford, Delhaize, etc.). The company reports results in two key segments, the United States at 60% of Q1 2025 net sales and Europe at 40%:

The company's enterprise value totals $49 billion, of which 68% is funded with common equity, leaving a 32% contribution for net debt. We should note that loans account for only 9% of enterprise value, with the remainder of net debt due to lease liabilities.

Q1 2025 results overview

The United States segment's net sales increased 5.2% Y/Y (1.8% in constant currency terms), powered by higher online sales, up 21.8% (17.9% in constant currency). The underlying operating margin slipped by ~0.3% Y/Y to 4.4%, impacted by low margin online and pharmacy sales. As a result, the segment's underlying operating income declined by 0.9% Y/Y (4.1% in constant currency).

Europe segment sales increased 10.1% Y/Y, largely due to the acquisition of Romanian grocery retailer Profi, which closed in the quarter. Excluding this effect, comparable sales increased 3.7% Y/Y. The underlying operating margin increased ~0.3% to 3.4%, driven by strong performance in Benelux. The net effect was a 19.9% Y/Y increase in underlying operating income.

On a consolidated basis, sales increased 7.1% (5% in constant currency), the underlying operating margin slipped by ~0.1% to 3.8%, and the underlying operating income increased by 3.3%. Underlying earnings per share growth was marginally stronger, increasing 4.6% Y/Y to €0.62/share, thanks to ongoing share repurchases:

Overall, I think it is encouraging to see Ahold Delhaize achieving robust online sales growth of 15.4% Y/Y. That said, these are clearly eroding the company's margins. Case in point, the underlying operating income margin stood at 4.3% in 2022, 4.1% in 2023, and 4% in 2024 before slipping to 3.8% in Q1 2025.

Outlook confirmed

Despite the weak margin performance early in 2025, the company did confirm its full-year guidance, expecting a circa 4% underlying operating margin for the full year and free cash flow of at least €2.2 billion:

Most notably, the outlook assumes a EUR/USD exchange rate of 1.10 for the full year. Should current margin weakness/euro strength prove persistent, odds are Ahold Delhaize faces downside risks related to its 2025 outlook. As a reminder, free cash flow stood at €2.4 billion in 2023 and €2.5 billion in 2024, with the +€2.2 billion target for 2025 falling below performance in recent years. As such, despite ongoing sales growth, I think the company will only achieve free cash flows of about €2.3 billion in 2025.

Valuation relative to Kroger

Taking my €2.3 billion free cash flow estimate for 2025, we see that the equity free cash flow yield stands at roughly 6.8%, with each €100 million in free cash flow representing roughly 0.3% in free cash flow yield to equity. Indeed, this is only marginally above Kroger's circa 6.4% free cash flow yield.

However, Ahold Delhaize is spending more on capex, around 3% of sales, compared to only 2.6% capex relative to sales spending at Kroger. This should allow Ahold Delhaize to achieve higher earnings growth in the future.

Last but not least, Ahold Delhaize holds marginally less debt compared to Kroger. Case in point, Ahold Delhaize's financial debt accounts for 9% of enterprise value, compared to circa 21% net debt contribution to enterprise value at Kroger:

Metric\company Ahold Delhaize Kroger
Free cash flow yield to equity, May 2025 6.8% 6.4%
Capex spending relative to sales 2025 3% 2.6%
Net financial debt relative to enterprise value (excluding lease liabilities), May 2025 9% 21%

Source: Author calculations

As such, even after the relative outperformance so far in 2025, I think Ahold Delhaize offers marginally better value compared to Kroger, although the difference is less pronounced compared to my previous article. Even so, I confirm my Buy rating for Ahold Delhaize.

Valuation relative to Carrefour

Ahold Delhaize remains more expensive compared to Carrefour. For instance, Carrefour offers a free cash flow yield to equity of about 15.7%, although it does come with lower capex, only 2.1% relative to sales, and higher net debt (29% of enterprise value):

Metric\company Ahold Delhaize Carrefour
Free cash flow yield to equity, May 2025 6.8% 15.7%
Capex spending relative to sales 2025 3% 2.1%
Net financial debt relative to enterprise value (excluding lease liabilities), May 2025 9% 29%

Source: Author calculations

As such, if you don't mind Carrefour's Brazil/Argentina exposure (which is 25% of Q1 2025 sales), you are most likely better off holding Carrefour over the medium term. On a standalone basis, I would definitely rate Carrefour a Buy.

However, since Europe is not the main market for Ahold Delhaize (i.e. Carrefour is not the best peer), I think sticking with my Buy rating on Ahold Delhaize (when compared to Kroger) is most prudent.

Risks

Looking back to my previous article, it appears Ahold Delhaize's margin weakness is more structural and unlikely to reverse soon. This may result in a prolonged period of weak free cash flow growth, as we have seen since 2023. Eventually, though, the company's high capex spending relative to competitors should pay off, allowing it to stabilize margins and return to free cash flow growth.

Should current dollar weakness continue, Ahold Delhaize's future free cash flow growth will also be impacted in the years ahead. Indeed, if you look at EUR/USD futures, you will see that interest rate differentials predict a circa 2% annual weakening of the U.S. dollar relative to the euro.

Last but not least, my Buy rating is largely based on a marginal discount relative to competitors such as Kroger in Ahold Delhaize's key U.S. market. As shown in the table for Carrefour above, it is quite likely that Carrefour outperforms Ahold Delhaize going forward, even when we factor in the exposure to Latin America.

Conclusion

Ahold Delhaize delivered weak Q1 2025 margins, impacted by growth in low-margin pharmacy and online sales. The company did confirm its full-year guidance, although it most notably assumes an average EUR/USD exchange rate below current levels, pointing to downside risks should current Euro strength continue.

I still think Ahold Delhaize offers marginally better value relative to Kroger, largely thanks to a higher free cash flow yield (despite Ahold Delhaize's higher capex spending) and lower leverage. As such, I confirm my Buy rating for Ahold Delhaize.

Investors should consider risks related to persistent margin weakness, future dollar depreciation relative to the euro, as well as the more compelling valuation of Europe-focused peers such as Carrefour.

Thank you for reading.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Edgewise Therapeutics: Upcoming DMD Trial Data May Disappoint
data.description:
“Etymologically, patient means sufferer. It is not suffering as such that is most deeply feared but suffering that degrades.”

Susan Sontag,

Illness as Metaphor

Introduction

Edgewise Therapeutics is a mid-cap, pre-revenue biotech focused on muscle diseases. The pipeline features two lead indications—DMD and HCM—and in both cases the strategy is similar: reduce muscle strain to preserve function.

Recent share-price swings were driven by oHCM data, but our analysis concentrates on DMD. The key catalyst is the long-delayed read-out for sevasemten (EDG-5506), now promised for Q2 2025. We do not expect material oHCM updates before then, whereas a negative DMD result—as we anticipate—would likely trigger a sharp sell-off. After outlining our case on sevasemten, we briefly review the oHCM program for completeness.

Thesis

We estimate Edgewise Therapeutics could fall roughly 50% if the upcoming DMD read-out disappoints. Even after the post-phase-1 oHCM haircut, the shares still trade well above cash, implying an enterprise value of about $900M (post-April raise). We contend most of that valuation rests on the DMD/BMD franchise; oHCM prospects remain uncertain, and the stock now hovers near pre-rally levels from late 2024. If DMD data underwhelm, the market will likely erase much of that EV. Assuming the DMD program accounts for 50–75 % of current value, a negative read-out should translate into roughly a 50% [-14%; -58%] drawdown, range estimated based on EV contribution DMD:HCM sensitivity analysis.

We believe that:

1. Sevasemten’s mechanism lacks convincing support from in-vivo human BMD data.

2. Prior attempts at muscle preservation in DMD have shown biomarker or lean-mass gains without functional benefit.

3. The drug’s MOA may introduce unwanted physiological trade-offs that offset any theoretical advantage.

4. The delay and “complexity of interpretation of biomarker data” in the dose finding study are poor predictors.

BMD and DMD

Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD) stems from mutations in the dystrophin gene (Duan 2021). Dystrophin is a massive structural protein (~427 kDa) with multiple isoforms; loss of the muscle isoform drives the disease’s severe morphologic and functional deficits. The protein spans from the sarcolemma to the actin cytoskeleton and anchors the fiber to the extracellular matrix, while its long central portion binds several other proteins essential to muscle physiology, and ameliorates strain (Moretti 2024)(Mias-Lucquin 2019).

DMD is rare—about 1 in 5,000 live-born boys (X-linked inheritance)—yet its clinical course varies because the label covers a spectrum of dystrophin mutations (appendix). Out-of-frame mutations yield little or no protein and produce the classic severe phenotype; in-frame mutations allow some truncated protein and manifest as the milder Becker muscular dystrophy (BMD), with a few caveats. DMD typically presents at 1–3 years, and natural-history data show loss of ambulation by ~10 years, ventilatory dependence around 20, and median life expectancy of 30–40 years, usually from cardiomyopathy. Becker progression is slower, with a potential near-normal life span in case of mild or well-controlled cardiac disease (Bushby 1993). Importantly, DMD survival has improved—and continues to improve—thanks to earlier diagnosis and widespread ventilator use.

For decades the backbone of DMD care has been corticosteroids, aimed at immunosuppression and slowing fibrotic remodeling. Steroids delay—but do not stop—disease progression and carry substantial long-term toxicities, fueling an intense search for better options. Recent approvals include exon-skipping oligonucleotides, gene therapy, and histone-deacetylase inhibitors. While these agents expand choices, most were cleared on surrogate endpoints; none yet shows unequivocal functional benefit.

EDG-5506 (Sevasemten)

To begin our analysis of sevasemten in dystrophinopathies, we first review its mechanism of action, which will guide our interpretation of the clinical data. According to the sponsor, sevasemten is a fast-myofiber myosin inhibitor that reduces ATPase activity through allosteric binding. Fast-twitch muscle fibers differ from slow-twitch fibers in several key respects, as outlined below (Biga 2019).

Figure 1. Muscle fiber types and characteristics (Biga 2019).

Fast-twitch fibers power brief, high-tension bursts of activity, whereas slow-twitch fibers handle lower-tension contractions over longer periods. Most muscles contain both, but the type I : type II ratio differs by muscle group and can shift with age, growth stage, and ethnicity (Schiaffino 2011)(Ama 1985).

Because muscle performance drives disability in DMD and BMD, it seems intuitive that preserving or boosting muscle mass should yield clinical benefit. Reality is less accommodating: multiple pre-clinically “sound” compounds aimed at muscle preservation have failed to improve function. At best, sevasemten could slow functional decline—or, optimistically, halt it (e.g. in Becker). Even if effective, the outcome would likely fall between those two scenarios since the drug only reduces contractile strain rather than eliminating the underlying defect (assuming strain drives degeneration).

To test that premise, we review Edgewise’s BMD biomarker and functional data and comment on the sponsor’s use of historical controls and statistical framing.

Creatine Kinase

Creatine Kinase (CK) is an enzyme abundantly present in muscle, where it catalyzes the conversion of creatine to phosphocreatine (Wallimann 1992). Because of this localization, CK is routinely measured in blood assays to detect damage to CK-rich muscle tissue. Elevated CK, however, occurs in both health and disease: levels can stay high for days after vigorous exercise and show baseline variation across racial groups (Johnsen 2010).

In DMD, a high CK is often one of the first diagnostic clues, but its lack of specificity requires confirmation with other tests—including genetic panels (see appendix). FDA guidance for DMD/BMD drug development—first issued in 2018 and updated in 2024 (McDonald 2024, supplement p. 92, p. 102)—warns that CK is “(…) not suitable as a primary endpoint in the ambulant population, as its value may vary depending not only on muscle mass but activity of the subject, with exertion causing significant elevations in comparison to baseline in DMD patients.” More broadly, the agency notes that muscle-injury biomarkers “may flag the deterioration of muscle seen in DMD, they may not correlate with performance and may not have prognostic value. However, some may provide additional information (including response to treatment and disease progression).” These cautions are particularly relevant here: the sponsor provided presentation (p. 13) lists "ambulatory males aged 12 to 50" as key inclusion criteria.

CK is further limited by its natural decline over time—a point Edgewise itself acknowledges in the 10-K (p. 12): “Systemically circulating biomarkers of myofiber degeneration, such as CK and troponin I are significantly elevated from birth with maximal levels at approximately two years of age. Levels decrease with time, presumably as muscle mass is lost.”

In the placebo-controlled Canyon trial (NCT05291091), 40 adults and 29 adolescents received 10 mg oral sevasemten or matching placebo daily for 12 months. Yet the sponsor reported only adult data. Slide 20 of Edgewise’s 2024 topline presentation highlights an ~30% CK reduction (p < 0.05); that headline figure warrants closer scrutiny, as discussed below.

Figure 2. – Canyon trial CK reduction (sponsor presentation, annotated with red).

A nominally impressive headline—p < 0.05 and nearly a 30% CK reduction—might look like a clear win. Well, not exactly. Two issues, both flagged by the sponsor’s own red arrows and circles, undercut the claim.

First, the endpoint. The slide reports the average CK change from months 6 to 12, yet both ClinicalTrials.gov and an earlier 2024 deck (p. 31) list the primary endpoint simply as "Change from Baseline in serum Creatine Kinase - 12 Months" and “CK at 12 months”, respectively. In other words, what was presented is not the endpoint originally listed.

Figure 3. – Canyon trial primary endpoint (sponsor presentation and clinicaltrials.gov, fragments).

Whether this 6-to-12-month average was prespecified or introduced later—inside or outside the SAP—is unclear. The optics are poor and suggest possible goalpost shifting. Admittedly, averaging does smooth the sharp month-12 spike (even the FDA cautioned about such swings). As it stands, the evidence is not convincing; we still don’t know how much 10 mg sevasemten truly lowers plasma CK in BMD patients.

Second, the slide’s red arrows show far wider CK scatter in the placebo group than in the treatment arm. That extra variability likely stems from a marked baseline imbalance in disease severity, which the sponsor itself acknowledges.

Figure 4. – Canyon trial baseline characteristics (sponsor presentation, annotated with red).

The 6-point baseline gap in NSAA is extremely consequential, given that the study was powered to detect a ~1-point difference! The slide’s footnote underscores the imbalance: only one placebo patient was unable to rise from the floor, versus nine in the sevasemten arm. Placebo patients were plainly in better physical condition, which translates into potentially much higher CK variability. It is not quite apples to oranges, but it is at least Golden Delicious to Granny Smith. We therefore see the initial CK rise in the placebo cohort as potentially largely driven by greater physical activity. Just as important, CK values in both arms were essentially identical by month 12—raising serious doubts about sevasemten’s in-vivo impact. None of this means the drug is inert; consistent CK and troponin declines in other cohorts (DUNE, ARCH) and supportive animal work confirm target engagement. The issue is magnitude: the effect appears too modest to produce functional benefit.

To put sevasemten in context, consider previous attempts to modulate muscle viability in DMD and BMD. As Edgewise itself notes, “Regulating myofiber contraction in Duchenne patients is not a novel concept. Dantrolene, an inhibitor of the ryanodine receptor that modulates both fast and slow skeletal myofiber contraction was used in a small clinical trial in 1991 in Duchenne patients. Treatment was associated with a 3-fold reduction in CK and a trend favoring attenuation of decreases in function.” Dantrolene, acting via a different mechanism, likewise reduced biomarkers but—across just seven boys who served as their own controls—failed to deliver statistically significant functional gains, even compared to age-matched historical controls (Quinn 2013)(Bertorini 1991).

Myostatin inhibitors followed a similar path. Domagrozumab showed no muscle-volume edge over placebo and was abandoned after its phase 2 trial (Wagner 2020). RG6206 (taldefgrobep alfa) did increase lean mass (albeit modestly) but still failed in phase 2/3, prompting Roche to declare the “approach will not be successful” (Meglio 2019)(Wagner 2019). The phase 2/3 data back this up: NSAA declined almost identically across placebo and both dose groups (−2.99, −3.44, −2.41) (Muntoni 2024). Some suggest limited efficacy may stem from already low myostatin levels in DMD patients, leaving little to inhibit further (Duan 2021). β2-agonists tell the same story—more lean mass, no functional gain (Skura 2007). In short, “improving” or preserving muscle metrics may not necessarily translate to clinical benefit for DMD patients.

Functional Data

Biomarkers aside, the real test is functional benefit. In DMD, regulatory approvals tied to biomarkers have relied on dystrophin expression assays, not on blood CK or skeletal troponin. As noted earlier, the most we can reasonably expect from sevasemten is a slowdown—or at best a temporary halt—in functional decline. Genuine improvement is biologically implausible; by reducing contraction, the drug should, if anything, induce a slight weakness. Any observed uptick in function is therefore best interpreted as regression to the mean or a placebo effect. The illustrative graph below shows the theoretical response profile we would anticipate for a strain-reducing agent like sevasemten.

Figure 5. – Theoretical response to a muscle strain lowering agent in muscle deteriorative disease (Author generated).

As the illustrative distribution shows, a strain-reducing drug should simply flatten the downward slope—shifting the curve modestly left—without producing outright gains. Edgewise itself states in a 10-K that“[their] goal is to reduce contraction of susceptible muscle fibers by five to twenty percent.” The real question is how much functional benefit can come from cutting contractile tension on those “susceptible muscle fibers” by just 5–20%. Type II fibers—the drug’s target—ordinarily account for about 50% of total muscle, as previously mentioned. Yet in DMD that share can fall dramatically; some studies put type II representation as low as 18% (Wang 1999). Even using the sponsor’s top-end figure, a 20% tension reduction across only half the fibers amounts to roughly a 10% drop in overall muscle work—and less if type II proportion is lower. It is hard to see how such a modest biochemical effect could yield a clinically meaningful benefit, a view reinforced by the functional data already discussed. There seems to be an interesting trade-off associated with sevasemten dosing – on one hand, to lower the potential muscle deterioration, one would need to reduce the strain, and potentially result in drug induced weakness. The problem is that inducing weakness in a condition that leads to progressive weakness is unwarranted. If one does not induce weakness, how can one reduce the mechanical damage? You can’t have your cake and eat it. (We note several weaker points from the sponsor’s preclinical package in the appendix, but they are of secondary importance, so will not be discussed here.)

In its December 2024 Canyon deck, the sponsor includes a bar-plot of individual NSAA changes for every patient in both placebo and sevasemten arms, as shown infra.

Figure 7. – Canyon trial response pattern NSAA (sponsor presentation, annotated with red).

The response pattern indicates that sevasemten has no discernible effect on functional measures. In our opinion, any apparent patient “improvements” are clearly unrelated to treatment. If the drug were working as proposed, we would expect a visible flattening of the decline; instead, the placebo group “decliners” declined by -2.13 NSAA points (calculated based on figure 7; [-6,-3,-2,-2,-1,-1,-1,-1]/8) while the sevasemten arm “decliners” declined by -2.0—essentially identical (calculated based on figure 7; [-5,-3,-2,-2,-2,-2,-1,-1,-1,-1]/10). To illustrate what a genuine effect should look like, we generated an idealized response curve and overlaid it on the trial data: the divergence is obvious.

Figure 8. – Canyon trial theoretically expected response pattern vs. observed pattern (Author generated).

Response pattern aside, the results were not statistically significant. The nominal NSAA difference between placebo and sevasemten was 1.12 points, but the reported p-value was 0.16. The sponsor maintains that the placebo group declined more—consistent with “natural history”—and that a larger, better-powered study would detect a true effect.

Figure 9. – Canyon trial NSAA change from baseline (sponsor presentation, annotated with red).

Several issues undermine that “just-needs-more-power” defense. First, the graph itself illustrates baseline imbalance: patients starting with higher NSAA scores are likelier to regress, whereas those starting lower can drift upward. Second, NSAA variability—especially in ambulatory BMD—is considerable. Although the sponsor quotes an average annual decline of –1, short-interval test–retest variability can equal that entire point. Indeed, the very sources the sponsor cites show a mean change of –1 but a median of 0, indicating the distribution is right-skewed (Bello 2016)(Wel 2024)(Velde 2021).

Third, the study was not underpowered. The figure below—taken directly from Bello 2016, a natural-history paper the sponsor itself references—shows that detecting a 1-point NSAA difference requires roughly 20 patients per arm for 80% power. Edgewise enrolled 40 adults, observed a 1.12-point gap, and still came up short on significance.

Figure 10. – BMD trial power calculation (Bello 2016).

Assuming a true 1-point treatment effect, about 20 patients per arm yield 80 % power to detect it. Edgewise enrolled 40 adults, observed a 1.12-point gap, and still missed significance (albeit, we acknowledge the 2:1 randomization). The culprit is variance: the sponsor’s own plot shows ±1-point error bars in both arms—essentially the size of the purported benefit. If sevasemten genuinely slowed decline, variability in the treatment group should narrow; instead, both arms show broad scatter. That variance highlights how cohort-specific BMD progression is, making historical comparisons inherently fragile—especially in small studies.

We therefore attribute the modest NSAA “signal” to placebo effect plus regression to the mean, not drug action. If the few patients who improved on sevasemten—improvements the mechanism does not predict—were excluded post-hoc, the two NSAA curves would almost overlap. Variability is consistently high; the 24-month open-label ARCH data show the same extreme scatter (appendix). In sum, we remain convinced sevasemten is unlikely to confer functional benefit in BMD—or in the more severe DMD setting.

LYNX (and FOX) trial

In October 2022, Edgewise launched the phase 2 LYNX study—a 12-week, placebo-controlled trial in DMD intended to assess safety, PK, and muscle-damage biomarkers, followed by an open-label extension of up to nine months. Target enrollment was ≈27 children, with the goal of finding a dose that best lowered CK and TNNI2 and, in turn, improved function.

By October 2023 the design had already shifted: enrollment expanded, a new dose-escalation cohort was added, and another cohort was opened for boys not on corticosteroids. Simultaneously, the company began FOX, a second phase 2 trial in gene-therapy–treated DMD patients. FOX was originally set for ≈24 participants, the same 12-week blinded period, and an open-label extension of up to 56 weeks (p. 36). Both trials have since drifted. The Q4 2024 10-K shows LYNX’s open-label phase stretched to 33 months (vs. 9; p. 38) and FOX enrollment doubled to ≈48, with its extension lengthened to 21 months.

Timelines slipped in parallel. A February 2023 release guided to a Q4 2023 LYNX interim read-out. By October 2023, that moved to 1H 2024, with phase 3 penciled for 2H 2024. The FY 2024 release then promised “3-month dose-ranging data” in Q2 2024. In Q1 2024 the read-out slid to Q4 2024, and phase 3 to H1 2025. The FY 2024 statement pushed everything again: “data from the phase 2 LYNX and FOX … in the first half of 2025.” Net: a 12-week study first slated to read out in Q4 2023 is now >18 months late—78 weeks of delay. In our opinion, for a short trial, that is a conspicuous red flag.

In a 13 Jan 2025 deck, the sponsor explains: “Multi-dose strategy in LYNX, guided by regulatory input, added complexity in interpretation of biomarker and functional data.”

Figure 11. – Update on the LYNX and FOX developments (sponsor presentation, annotated with red).

“Multi-dose strategy in LYNX, guided by regulatory input, added complexity in interpretation of biomarker and functional data.” Having deemed 5 mg and 10 mg “potentially active,” the team added two more cohorts, treating them for six months (presumably still blinded and placebo-controlled) before re-evaluating biomarkers and function. Given the lack of convincing efficacy in BMD, we do not expect meaningful benefit in DMD—a more severe phenotype. Should these trials fail to show functional improvement, we would mark the DMD/BMD program at well under a 1% probability of success.

Note on EDG-7500 and o/nHCM data

EDG-7500 is a selective fast-myosin inhibitor aimed at cardiac muscle and is being tested in both obstructive and non-obstructive HCM. After the initial single-dose read-out in seven oHCM patients, the stock ran from ≈$20 to ≈$30 and peaked near $35 in November 2024. Then came topline part-B data: 17 patients treated for 28 days. Despite “positive” results—LVOT-gradient down, LVEF preserved—the shares plunged to ≈$10, wiping out the prior rally and more. Two linked issues explain the reaction. First, the safety profile was underwhelming; second, safety was supposed to be EDG-7500’s edge over mavacamten and aficamten.

Figure 12. – EDG-7500 safety profile in phase 1 trial (sponsor presentation).

Small early-phase trials are meant for dose-finding and general safety sweeps; they are usually too small to spot infrequent adverse events. So when a serious signal appears this early, it is bad news—programs are often shelved for less. In Part B, 13.8 % of patients developed atrial fibrillation—a red-flag rate in a cohort of just 17. That is especially troubling given that we believe that better safety—chiefly on LVEF—was supposed to differentiate EDG-7500 from mavacamten and aficamten.

The sponsor counters that (1) tighter phase-3 inclusion criteria will drive AF down and (2) the rate is “comparable” to competitors. But the drug was marketed as better, not merely comparable: mavacamten is already on the market, and Cytokinetics hopes aficamten will win approval without a boxed warning (PDUFA now December 2025 after the REMS delay). Add nearly 30 % dizziness, and the picture is less than reassuring. Little attention was paid to the non-obstructive arm, where three of five high-dose patients dropped out before week 4 (p. 30).

We remain skeptical. If dose is lowered to curb AEs, efficacy will likely slip below competitors; keep the dose and safety issues may block a favorable label. Mechanistically, the supposed advantage—targeting a different sarcomeric protein than current CMIs—may be more academic than practical. Whether you inhibit protein 1, 2, or 10 in the contraction cascade, the net result is reduced inotropy, leading to higher compliance and lower stroke volume. The sponsor says EDG-7500 facilitates relaxation and ventricular filling. Dog data do show decreased end-systolic and end-diastolic elastance, i.e., lower contractility and greater compliance—a pattern familial to one reported for mavacamten (Rio 2023)(Nag 2023).

Figure 12. – EDG-7500 vs. mavacamten pressure volume loops (Rio 2023, Nag 2023, fragments).

It is difficult to see how EDG-7500 could truly show less LVEF depression than mavacamten or aficamten unless it delivers markedly different contractile sparing. The regression plots the sponsor touts—comparing plasma concentrations across EDG-7500, mavacamten, and aficamten—ignore a key point: EDG-7500 likely has a far higher molar mass, so nanogram-per-milliliter levels are not directly comparable without proper molar adjustment. Edgewise has never disclosed the compound’s structure, but a review of recent patent filings (WO2024206345A1, WO2024073426A1, WO2024206339A1, WO2024206347A1) suggests the molecule’s mass could be roughly double that of mavacamten or aficamten. Without head-to-head data, any claim of mechanistic superiority remains largely speculative.

Valuation & Cash Burn

Each asset in development must be valued on its own, making a sum-of-the-parts calculation for Edgewise tricky. The HCM market is sizeable: despite a boxed warning, mavacamten generated $159 M in Q1 2025, up 89 % YoY after its April 2022 launch. Merck touts multi-billion potential, but that depends on label expansion, reduced monitoring, and limited direct competition. MyoKardia’s $13.1 B acquisition in 2020 shows a successful HCM drug can command a lofty price. Still, as additional agents enter the field, only those offering clear safety or efficacy advantages will capture share; others will split revenue—or fail outright. Given EDG-7500’s early stage and safety questions, we view its probability of success in the high-single to low-double digits.

By contrast, we assign virtually no value to the DMD/BMD franchise. The BMD data show no meaningful efficacy, and we expect the delayed LYNX and FOX phase 2 readouts to confirm (i) no credible dose–response on functional measures and (ii) no differentiation from placebo.

Accordingly, we place Edgewise’s fair value at or only slightly above cash—about $5–7 per share. As of Q1 2025, the company reported $436 M in cash, equivalents, and marketable securities and no debt; a subsequent equity raise pushed the figure to ≈$660 M. Operating expenses have been heavy: $35M (Q124), $38M (Q224), $40M (Q324), $45M (Q424) and $46M (Q125). With such level of expenditure, Edgewise appears to be adequately funded in the medium term.

Risks

Several risks could upend our thesis. First, we may be underestimating EDG-7500’s true value: phase-3 trials could reveal a lower incidence of atrial fibrillation and dizziness, prompting a sharp re-rating. Second, the sponsor plans to release 12-week data from the phase-2 HCM study; if DMD readouts slip again, positive HCM results could arrive first and drive the stock higher. Third, even a marginal DMD outcome might be marketed as a win and spark a rally. Fourth, as a mid-cap biotech, Edgewise is inherently volatile, and swings in sector sentiment can move the shares irrespective of fundamentals. Finally, the FDA could exercise additional flexibility for a rare disease like DMD.

Conclusion

Edgewise’s value hinges on two main assets—EDG-7500 and sevasemten. In the near term, we expect the postponed phase-2 readouts from the LYNX and FOX DMD trials to drive the share price. We anticipate these studies will fail to demonstrate convincing efficacy, likely triggering a sharp valuation decline. While the stock has already sold off following the HCM phase-2 data, we believe further downside remains.

data.title: A Tale Of Two Markets: Gold Vs. U.S. Stocks
data.description:
With the PE ratio of the S&P 500 index near all-time highs (particularly relative to real interest rates), the pricing of US stocks is implicitly reflecting exceptional optimism about the prospects for future economic growth, combined with extraordinary complacency about potential macroeconomic risks.

By contrast, with the purchasing power of gold near an all-time high (400+ years), the pricing of gold implicitly reflects exceptional pessimism about long-term macroeconomic conditions and great anxiety about the potential for future inflation.

These drastically contrasting visions about the future that are represented in the price of US equities and gold remind me of two worlds that simultaneously coexisted somewhat uneasily in Charles Dickens' classic novel, A Tale of Two Cities. In this novel, Dickens’ vision of Paris represented a cautionary tale about the latent potential for the collapse of a great and prosperous nation due to internal contradictions. By contrast, Dickens' portrayal of British society represented optimism about the potential for London to avoid the fate of Paris.

Given Dickens' keen knowledge of the deplorable conditions of the lower classes in London at the time of that A Tale of Two Cities was written, one wonders why the author seems to be relatively hopeful about the future of the monarchical and aristocratic society of England, given some very clear parallels that he drew between Victorian Britain and the doomed ancien regime of France. This is a seeming contradiction that is without a ready explanation.

In this article I am going to explore a different, and perhaps even more difficult apparent contradiction: How is it that in efficient capital market, the pricing of one asset can express views about the macroeconomy that fundamentally contradict the views being expressed in the pricing of another asset? Specifically, the question I wish to explore is: How is it possible that in an integrated capital market, the price of gold can reflect a deep pessimism about the future while the price of equities reflects such a radical optimism? Wouldn't arbitrage cause the pricing of different assets to converge toward compatible views?

After we answer these questions, we conclude with a reflection on how unravelling this paradox in financial markets might enable a reader to gain some insight that could explain the apparent contradiction that is at the heart of A Tale of Two Cities.

An Insight into Pricing Theory

The key to understanding the apparent contradiction between the pricing of gold and the pricing of equities lies in the integration of two key insights that are derived from the theory of price formation.

The first insight is that the market price of any asset is not determined by the “average” opinion of market participants with regards to its fair value. It is determined by the opinion of the marginal buyer and seller of that particular asset.

If the pricing of assets were determined by the average opinion of all market participants, then the visions of the macroeconomic future that are implicitly expressed in the prices of gold and US equities would be entirely consistent. But the pricing of assets in financial markets is never determined by average opinion; pricing is determined by the opinions of marginal participants. And, as we shall see, the marginal participants in the markets for US equities and gold are fundamentally different.

The second insight necessary to resolve the apparent contradiction reflected in the prices of gold and US equities is this: All assets are always owned by the highest bidders. Stated differently, assets are always owned by the most optimistic fringe (regarding the prospects for that asset) among all market participants. This means that the pricing of any asset always reflects the opinions of a narrow fringe of the most optimistic market participants with regards to that asset.

Now, let’s apply this insight to the pricing of gold and US equities.

The Price of Equities is Determined by Optimists

In accordance with the fundamental microeconomic principle just described above, every single stock that exists is always owned by the market participants that value it most highly. This means that the valuation of the S&P 500 on aggregate, always reflects the most wildly optimistic valuations of each individual stock that comprises the index. The end result is that, on aggregate, the valuation of an index such as the S&P 500 will generally reflect a valuation that most market participants will deem to be unreasonably high. This “unreasonably high” valuation paradoxically projects an optimistic opinion about the future regarding aggregate growth and profitability of US companies that very few of the shareholders of the companies that comprise the index actually share.

In other words, if you polled the shareholders of individual companies that comprise the S&P 500 index regarding their opinions regarding broad economic conditions and broader profitability trends, they would overwhelmingly tend to have opinions that are less optimistic than the valuation of the overall S&P 500 index projects.

This creates a paradox where the market valuation of the aggregate index does not accurately represent the macroeconomic views of the shareholders of the individual companies that comprise the index. The macro opinion projected by the valuation of the index is always far more optimistic than the opinions of the average participant and/or the average of opinions of the participants.

But it is crucial to understand a critical distinction: The high valuation of an index such as the S&P 500 is not a function of the fact that the owners of equities are optimistic about the economy or the overall market. They are only optimistic about the specific stocks that they own. But since the price of each stock is always determined by the most optimistic market participants, the aggregate value of the overall market will always project an implied optimism that far exceeds the actual assessments of the majority of market participants.

The Price of Gold Is Determined by Pessimists

The extremely optimistic opinion regarding the future of the macroeconomy that is projected in the aggregate price of equities has a mirror-image counterpart in the pricing of gold. The pricing of gold projects the most optimistic opinions about gold. But the most optimistic opinions about gold are driven by those that are most pessimistic about the future of the macroeconomy and the stock market. People that are reasonably optimistic about the future do not tend to be marginal buyers of gold. The marginal buyers of gold are always those that are so pessimistic about the future that they are willing to outbid all potential buyers in the market.

Therefore, the price of gold always reflects the opinions of the most pessimistic fringe among market participants.

What is Different Now?

Everything about the pricing of equities and gold which I described above has always true and will always be true. These basic facts have never changed and will never change. These insights are derived from fundamental microeconomic principles. So, what is different now? What enables us to explain the particularly extraordinary divergence of opinions that are currently projected by the pricing of equities and gold?

The difference is this: The optimistic fringe of market participants has almost never been as optimistic as they are now, and the pessimistic fringe has almost never been as pessimistic as they are now. There has been a polarization and a radicalization of opinion at the fringes of the market. Therefore, when you put these two facts together with the insights about price formation that I have laid out, it becomes apparent why the divergence in the opinions reflected in gold pricing and equity pricing are greater than they have ever been.

Why Is There Such a Radical Difference of Opinion?

It is important to remember that, in trying to reconcile the opinions implicitly projected in the pricing of equities and gold, it makes virtually no difference what the opinions are of average market participants. In the pricing of assets that trade in financial markets, the opinions of the marginal participants are the only ones that are relevant.

In this essay, I cannot go into depth about all of the reasons for the polarization and radicalization of opinions at the margins of financial markets. However, I can provide a general outline of an answer that should suffice.

The first reason that I can point to is related to the polarization of political and/or ideologically-based opinions about the future of the US economy. This is a well-documented development in the political science literature. It is, therefore, not surprising to see this polarization and radicalization of opinions about the future of the US economy at the fringes among financial markets participants.

Second, I believe that the polarization that is observed in opinions about the future of the US economy can be explained in large part due to the highly polarized opinions on two specific topics: 1) Artificial intelligence. 2) National debt.

Regarding artificial intelligence, the pricing of US equities is becoming increasingly dominated by the opinions of the most optimistic fringe of market participants on the topic of AI. While there are many skeptics and even pessimists regarding AI – indeed, they may even constitute a majority of market participants -- their opinions are irrelevant to market pricing of individual stocks. The pricing of every US stock is ultimately dominated by the most optimistic market participants. And since owners of most US stocks are almost necessarily the most optimistic about what AI will do for the growth and profitability of the stocks they own, then it will tend to be their very optimistic views of the impact of AI on their own individual stocks that will be projected in the aggregate valuation of the market.

Regarding the national debt, this issue hardly registers at all among the most optimistic equity investors. In other words, if you ask the owners of individual stocks (who are necessarily the most optimistic market participants regarding that stock), it is likely that most of them will say that the national debt has played no role in their valuation of that stock. By contrast, if you ask the marginal investor of gold what factors led them to buy gold, they will probably cite the US national debt as one of the top reasons (if not the single most important reason). Therefore, as the national debt has become an increasingly pressing issue, it is no surprise that pessimism on this issue has become visibly reflected in the price of gold.

Paradox Solved

In sum, there is no fundamental economic contradiction reflected in the pricing of equities and gold. The paradox dissolves when we realize that the marginal buyer of gold is more pessimistic than ever about the factors that influence the pricing of gold (e.g. national debt) and the marginal buyer of equities is more optimistic than ever about the factors that tend to drive the valuation of equities (e.g. impact of artificial intelligence).

Interpreting Dickens’ Tale of Two Cities in Light of Our Tale of Two Assets

It is obviously beyond the scope of this article to provide a full interpretation of A Tale of Two Cities. However, I think we can apply one insight explored in this article to the analysis of the situation affecting pre-revolutionary France and England in the novel – and Victorian England at the time that the novel was published. Specifically, we want to know: What explains the relative optimism that is implicit in the novel regarding the future of England in light of the events of revolutionary France? We think that this apparent contradiction can, in part, be explained as follows:

In France, the French citizens that were most pessimistic about their own future wanted revenge and a violent revolution, even if it was at the cost of their own lives. This is a functional equivalent of the most pessimistic market participants demanding gold and driving its price up to levels that project a crisis.

In Britain, even the Englishmen that were most pessimistic about the future of their own country dreamed that they themselves might one day marry, have children and perhaps buy shares of stock on the London Stock Exchange. This is the functional equivalent of many investors that might be somewhat pessimistic about the economic future of the USA, but are reasonably optimistic about the future of the stocks that they own.

A more fundamental difference between the "marginal participants" of two societies can scarcely be imagined. In my view, it is fundamentally this difference of mindset among the “marginal participants” of the societies of pre-revolutionary France and England which ultimately told the "tale of two cities."

Application to Current Market Outlook

The application of the principles illustrated in this essay to the current market outlook are somewhat evident, upon reflection.

First, despite the extremely widespread belief that the overall equity market is “overvalued,” we can predict that its value will not decline significantly unless and until the most optimistic fringe among market participants become more pessimistic than they are currently. In practice, this would require a shift in the mindset of this most optimistic cohort of market participants. Specifically, we believe that fears of a cyclical recession would need to begin to (at least temporarily) over-ride the long-term optimism about AI that has taken hold of their imaginations.

Regarding the outlook for gold, it is quite evident that the most pessimistic fringe among market participants has become convinced that the US is on the verge of a debt crisis. However, it is a characteristic of human nature that the intensity of pessimism about the future is rarely maintained for very long. If and when the immediacy and/or urgency of this fear wears off (at least temporarily), the marginal demand for gold will wane -- particularly in light of the extremely high price of gold relative to virtually all other goods and services. A decline in the price of gold does not require that the flow of bad news about debts and deficits diminish. All that is required is that the flow of bad news remain of the same nature and at roughly the same rate.

It is our view that changes in the mindsets of marginal participants in both gold and equity markets will occur at some point during the remainder of 2025. We expect these changes to cause a significant downward adjustment in the prices of US equities and gold – although not necessarily at the same exact points in time.

Final Thoughts

At Successful Portfolio Strategy, we have a unique fundamental understanding of how the pricing of assets work. The pricing of assets does not only depend on a correct interpretation of fundamentals. It also depends on a correct interpretation of who the marginal buyers and sellers are and what their behaviors are likely to be given the fundamental environment. By having a unique understanding of both fundamentals and key asset pricing principles, we are able to identify opportunities that almost everybody else misses. For example, in late 2024, we were able to predict that both US equities and gold would likely rise in price, despite generally being uncorrelated, due to the fact that the election of Donald Trump would likely increase marginal demand for equities and gold. We also anticipated that equities would likely rise despite a likely increase in Treasury yields – despite the fact that consensus of opinion would typically predict the opposite. Our reasoning was that the marginal investor in bonds tends to be sensitive to inflation risk and increasing debt in a way that the marginal equity investor is not. This manner of analysis also proved to be the key to understanding why gold would rise despite rising bond yields (historical correlations of treasury yields and gold tend to be highly negative). In this case, our insight was that higher bond yields would be offset by an explosion of pessimism among marginal investors about debt and inflation.

Fundamentals matter. But they act through the medium of the opinions and behavior of marginal buyers and sellers.

data.title: TriplePoint Venture Growth: 18% Dividend Yield Is A Red Flag (Downgrade)
data.description:
TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC (NYSE:TPVG) slashed its dividend in the third quarter of 2024 and since then, the business development company has exhibited softening dividend payout metrics, which could put even the lower dividend at risk again of a second cut.

TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC has consistently underperformed in terms of its non-accruals and has racked up losses from its investment portfolio in the last year on a near continuous basis.

Unfortunately, my previous optimism about TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC has proven to have been unfounded, and I think that a lower stock classification 'Hold' is appropriate taking the heightened investment risks.

My last stock classification on TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC was 'Buy', primarily because the business development company experienced a major valuation haircut that was directly related to its high degree of investment losses that crashed the company's net assets and net investment income.

The non-accrual ratio is not improving as much as I had hoped and with the margin of dividend safety narrowing again in the last two quarters, I think that another dividend may be on the horizon for TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC.

Non-Accruals And Investment Losses Reflect Investment Risks

TriplePoint Venture Growth is offering capital to venture-backed technology companies and therefore has considerable overlap, in its strategic direction, with other business development companies like Hercules Capital Inc. (HTGC) and Horizon Technology Finance (HRZN) both of which serve the same market segment of middle-market companies.

TriplePoint Venture Growth owns primarily debt investments, a total of $565.4 million to be precise, which is supplemented by equity and warrant investments that had a total value of $116.6 million.

Equity and warrants therefore account for a whopping 17.1% of the business development company's investment portfolio as of March 31, 2025, making the BDC reliant on strong equity performance moving forward.

TriplePoint Venture Growth does not have the best investment record, and it is showing in the BDC's net asset value, which has decreased a whopping 38% since Q4 2021, predominantly because its debt losses have surged. In the last five quarters, the business development company has seen a steady stream of troubled loans that hurt TriplePoint Venture Growth's net asset value as well as net investment income performance.

Since Q1 2024, TriplePoint Venture Growth has produced $4.1 million in net realized and unrealized losses each quarter, which is mainly due to an underperforming credit portfolio, characterized by an above average level of non-accruals.

High losses, both realized and unrealized, have taken a substantial bite out of the BDC's net asset value in recent years, leading to a peer group-leading discount to net asset value.

TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC has been plagued by above average non-accruals and investment losses that have seriously dented the business development company's performance, particularly last year.

As of March 31, 2025, TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC had a non-accrual ratio of 3.6% (shown in the chart below as a % of the debt investment portfolio at fair value) which was down 0.1 percentage points QoQ.

Though the performance trend in impaired loans shows signs of improvement, as non-accruals appear to have peaked in Q2 2024 at 8.2%, the business development company still has to deal with a higher amount of impaired loans than the average BDC.

Hercules Capital, for instance, had a non-accrual ratio of only 0.5% in the March-quarter and therefore offers passive income investors a substantially enhanced credit profile.

Payout Metrics

TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC slashed its dividend by 25% to $0.30 per share in the third quarter of 2024 amid a surge in investment losses and a simultaneous decrease in net investment income.

In Q1 2025, the business development company earned only $0.27 in net investment income from its debt investments, thus falling $0.03 short of the $0.30 per share per quarter dividend in the payout ratio.

Only three quarters after the BDC adjusted its dividend lower, TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC's dividend payout ratio again rose above 100% in Q1 2025.

Hercules Capital and Horizon Technology Finance are two other business development companies that have the same market strategy and serve the same customers. Hercules Capital, on an LTM basis, had the best dividend payout ratio of 81% while Horizon Technology Finance could also be staring down a dividend cut with an LTM payout of 108%.

Serious Discount To NAV Reflects Heightened Dividend Risk

Ultra-high dividend yields in the BDC industry very rarely last for a long time, as they are more often than not are a reflection of underlying credit and dividend coverage problems. In the case of TPVG, the stock presently yields 18%, a clear red flag if you asked me.

TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC is selling for the highest discount to net asset value for reasons mentioned above. Presently, this discount sits at 22%, reflecting the risk of an imminent dividend cut.

Horizon Technology Finance, whose dividend is not the safest either, is selling for 0.99x net asset value, whereas Hercules Capital is getting the only premium in the BDC peer group with a Price-To-NAV ratio of 1.49x.

In my view, the present discount to TPVG's net asset value properly reflects the risk of a second dividend cut.

Hercules Capital, however, has earned a reputation of being extremely meticulous and diligent in its loan originations and the dividend is regularly covered well by the BDC's net investment income.

Not surprisingly, Hercules Capital also has the highest Price-To-NII ratio of 9.7x whereas both TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC and Horizon Technology Finance sell for substantially less, 6-7x NII.

Why TPVG Might Sell For An Even Bigger NAV Discount

Business development companies have gotten clobbered in the last two months, though we have seen valuation recoveries, particularly for BDCs that have strong credit histories and good dividend coverage. Despite market-driven volatility, there are riskier and less risky choices for BDCs as investment vehicles, and TPVG is definitely a higher-risk BDC.

In my view, TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC has considerable dividend adjustment risk in the short term, which is why I am lowering my stock classification back to 'Hold.'

If TPVG indeed slashed its dividend a second time in a short period of time, I think that the stock could be in for more pain and possibly sell for a much bigger discount to net asset value.

My Takeaway

TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC is a struggling business development company as the BDC's dividend payout metrics continued to soften in the last two quarters. This is highly unfortunate, taking into account that TPVG already slashed its dividend last year by 25% in order to realign its dividend payout with lower net investment income.

The business development company still has a very high non-accrual ratio of 3.6% which is unacceptable, particularly with BDC like Hercules Capital providing a stark contrast in terms of credit quality.

The dividend has not been covered by net investment income in Q1 2025, and the margin of safety has complete vanished, suggesting that TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC might have to announce a second dividend cut. Hold.

data.title: Teleperformance: Supportive Momentum Ongoing And AI Upside
data.description:
Following the Q1 release of Teleperformance (OTCPK:TLPFF) (OTCPK:TLPFY), we are back to comment on the market leader in the outsourced customer experience. Our buy rating was supported by 1) a more Specialized Services company with higher EBITDA evolution 2) AI Upside To Price In, 3) market mispriced of Majorel's latest acquisition, underestimating its strategic value and long-term earnings potential and 4) >+14% free cash flow yield with downside protection due to an ongoing buyback and a >4% dividend yield. Since our last coverage (Q4 and FY 2024 results), Teleperformance's share price has remained flat, but the company has paid its dividend (Fig. 1), and the total return has reached a plus 5%.

Fig 1

Q1 Results and Our Positive Take

The company reported solid first-quarter results, with sales growth of +1.6% year-on-year to €2.6 billion. Cross-checking Wall Street consensus estimates, Teleperformance beat the average analyst's expectation, which was forecasting a +1.1% growth. This modest growth beat was led by encouraging trends in Core Services, but from a segment perspective, Specialised Services grew by 10.7% (Fig. 2). Despite a volatile operating environment and some known contract headwinds (TLS), the company managed to achieve solid sales momentum in Europe and the Middle East. Additionally, the sales growth in the Specialized Services division was supported by ZP integration. As a reminder, in Q1, the company did not disclose its margin and earnings; however, Teleperformance has reaffirmed its Fiscal Year 2025 guidance for +2-4% LFL growth, a group EBITA margin of 15.0-15.1% (Fig. 4), and a free cash flow of at least €1 billion.

Source: Teleperformance Q1 results ppt - Fig 2

Why are we still Positive?

  1. While much of the industry is focused on the growing role of AI in customer relationship management, there are essential counterexamples, most notably Klarna. Here at the Lab, we already commented on that. Interestingly, Klarna was one of the companies that triggered a sell-off in Teleperformance shares following news that Klarna would increasingly use AI to handle customer interactions. However, Klarna has changed course. The CEO recently made it clear that customers will always have the option to speak with a real person, and the company now plans to bring in more staff to strengthen its support team. This reflects how, for some customers, speaking with a human still matters a great deal. This is a positive upside for Teleperformance and cannot go unnoticed;
  2. Given the limited company's Q1 disclosure, we see support in the unchanged 2025 guidance, especially with the support of Specialized Services sales. It appears that Teleperformance is effectively integrating ZP. The company specializes in communication services tailored to the deaf and hard-of-hearing community (Fig. 3) —a niche market that smaller competitors may lack the resources or scale to serve effectively. Still related to M&A, Majorel integration is progressing well, and Teleperformance also reaffirmed its cost synergy target of €20-€30 million for the year. This comes in addition to the €94 million in synergies already realized in 2024. There still appears to be additional potential to extract further efficiencies as the integration continues;
  3. During the quarter, it entered into partnerships with two leading Agentic AI companies — Ema and Parloa — as part of its broader investment program, which is set to allocate up to €100 million in 2025. Beyond enhancing operational efficiency in addressing client needs, these collaborations could provide a competitive edge over rivals that have yet to adopt similar technologies. Furthermore, should these partnerships prove successful, Teleperformance retains the option to participate in the next funding rounds for both firms. These deals follow an earlier partnership with Sanas. The company is leveraging Sanas technology to neutralize the accents of customer service representatives based in India and the Philippines, aiming to enhance communication clarity and improve the customer experience. We expect the economics of this and itsimplications to be a focus at the upcoming CMD on 18th June;
  4. With the support of GEO sales, Teleperformance expanded its presence in the Euro area. Growth was well balanced across GEO, and management remains confident in an acceleration over the year.

Fig 3

Fig 4

Earnings Changes and Valuation

Last time, we reported a disruption in TLS Visa application sales, and we continue to forecast top-line sales of €10.8 billion. This is aligned with management guidance. Following the Q1 print, we should forecast higher growth in the Specialized Services segment. At this stage, we prefer to remain prudent and await further evidence at the upcoming Capital Market Day. B2B service spending is likely to come under some form of pressure, and there is also an adverse FX evolution to be priced in. That said, Teleperformance has much upside potential on ZP consolidation (which is already delivering) and Majorel cost synergies. We continue to believe the top management team is broadly cautious, especially on EBITA expansion. Continuing to apply a 15.1% margin, we reached €1.63 billion EBITA. With the new financing debt, we estimate an interest payment of at least €200 million per year; however, the company has €1 billion FCF potential for deleverage per year. In our base-case scenario, we continue to apply a €500 million buyback and a €500 million debt reduction. Applying a tax rate of 25.5% aligned with Teleperformance's historical average, we project a net profit forecast of €1.03 billion. Considering the current share numbers, Teleperformance EPS reached €17.2. This still does not assume the ongoing buyback.

At this stage, Teleperformance trades at an EV/EBITDA ratio of less than 5x and a P/E ratio of 5.23x. The company's current valuation remains attractive, even without considering the potential margin upside. We are somewhat surprised that the stock did not react more positively following the encouraging Klarna update, particularly considering how it has responded sharply to negative news in the past year. This is also well below Teleperformance's historical average (Figs. 5 and 6). With a double-digit free cash flow yield, for consistency reasons, we confirmed our 13x price-to-earnings target. Following a minor EPS evolution, our valuation is set at €223.60 per share ($126 in ADR). Thus, our Strong Buy Recommendation is sustained.

Fig 5

Fig 6

Risks and Threats

We have extensive coverage of Teleperformance, so we report the following downside risks: 1) decline in Specialized Services sales following the non-renewal of the TLScontact contract highlights a potential risk for the company; 2) new technology and incumbent players, 3) challenging pricing environment, 4) continued CAPEX investing and failure to adopt and effectively integrate emerging technologies, 5) client concentration risk, 6) wage inflation and FX volatility, 7) regulatory risks, for instance, in 2003, the company faced strict USA regulations regarding the "Do Not Call List," and 8) negative investor sentiment toward the sector.

Regarding a new risk, we report that the company has recently undertaken restructuring initiatives in both Spain and France for a total consideration of €54 million. This is already included in our estimates, but it may signal that the company is facing certain operational headwinds.

Conclusion

There appear to be two opposing views on GenAI’s impact on the customer experience industry: one suggesting it undermines the very foundation of the CX business model, and another viewing it as a new tool that companies in the sector will integrate, making the recent derating unjustified. We align with the latter perspective. In our view, the company is well-positioned to offer a hybrid solution, and with a compelling valuation discount, we reiterate our investment conviction.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Prices Re-Tethering To Income
data.description:
Ultra-luxury goods are often regarded as recession-proof, with steady demand regardless of the state of the economy. But that’s not what is happening.

During the pandemic, when ‘free money’ policies sent demand for goods through the roof, many luxury companies seized the opportunity, raising prices by an average of 36% between 2020 and 2023, according to Bernstein analysis (chart below)—roughly double the rate of overall U.S. inflation at the time. For a while, demand proved relatively inelastic, holding up despite soaring prices.

This was good for companies, where higher prices with steady demand increased total revenue. Now, as with unaffordable homes, cars and motor homes, ‘greedflation’ is coming back to bite in the form of weaker demand.

With excess inventory building in many sectors, cheaper options are taking market share from rivals that consumers now consider overpriced. That’s a problem for those who cannot or will not reduce prices, and then there is the matter of who will pay for new tariffs. See Luxury Brands Are Paying for Over-the-Top Price Hikes:

Luxury brands also can’t cut prices without damaging their image. They spend billions of dollars a year on advertising to convince shoppers to pay steep markups on their goods. Charging less is an admission that they have misread the market and that their goods aren’t as desirable as thought.

More likely, labels will sit on their hands until inflation and income growth slowly make their goods seem more affordable. They can also design new lower-priced products to boost sales.

Brands that showed restraint during the pandemic have the best chance of shielding their profit margins from President Trump’s tariffs.

…At the end of the day everything has its price, even luxury.

Disclosure: No positions.

Original Post
